citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
At the time of inspection, there was no written program of veterinary care available for review.
A completed written program of veterinary care is important for the care of the animals and ensures clear and accurate
communication between the facility and the attending vet.
Each exhibitor shall employ an attending veterinarian under formal arrangements. In the case of a part-time attending
veterinarian or consultant arrangements, the formal arrangements shall include a written program of veterinary care and
regularly scheduled visits to the premises of the exhibitor.
To be corrected by May 19, 2026.

During the review of records, the licensee was unable to provide complete acquisition and disposition records for birds.
Cage cards are used to document births; however, these records are discarded after rearing and are not maintained. The
facility tracks the number of birds sold but does not maintain records of birds that died or were euthanized.
Failure to maintain complete acquisition and disposition records interferes with the ability to accurately identify animals
and trace their movement.
The licensee shall make, keep, and maintain records that fully and correctly disclose information concerning regulated
animals purchased, born, hatched or otherwise acquired, as well as animals transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise
disposed.
To be corrected by May 19, 2026.

During the inspection, 6 budgies were observed outside their enclosures and flying free inside the Big Barn.
Enclosures that fail to safely contain birds may result in escape, injury, or death of the animal. Birds that are outside of
their enclosure are at increased risk of predation, possible restricted access to food, water, shelter, and potential non-
recovery.
Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they contain the birds securely.
To be corrected by May 19. 2026.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The facility failed to prevent the accumulation excreta and food waste from the primary enclosures housing birds. In one
enclosure containing a pair of breeding budgies the droppings were piled up the level of the perch (approximately 4
inches). In another enclosure there was a mound of fecal approximately 2 inches in diameter that was covered in a green
hairy mold-like substance. Approximately 70 percent of the enclosures in Willow barn and the Big Barn were covered with
a layer of caked-on excreta and food waste. There were approximately 10 cages with dead budgies in various states of
decay
Failure to clean accumulations of waste materials can increase disease hazards, attract insects, pests, and be a source of
odors.
Excreta and food waste must be removed from the primary enclosures and from under and from around primary
enclosures as often as necessary to prevent excessive accumulation of feces and food waste, to reduce disease hazards,
insects, pests, and odors.
To be corrected by May 19, 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
Geoffrey Olsen, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER
Emily Roberson n

narrative:
2.40(a)(1) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
At the time of inspection, there was no written program of veterinary care available for review.  
 
A completed written program of veterinary care is important for the care of the animals and ensures clear and accurate 
communication between the facility and the attending vet. 
 
Each exhibitor shall employ an attending veterinarian under formal arrangements. In the case of a part-time attending 
veterinarian or consultant arrangements, the formal arrangements shall include a written program of veterinary care and 
regularly scheduled visits to the premises of the exhibitor.  
To be corrected by May 19, 2026. 
2.75(b)(1) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
During the review of records, the licensee was unable to provide complete acquisition and disposition records for birds. 
Cage cards are used to document births; however, these records are discarded after rearing and are not maintained. The 
facility tracks the number of birds sold but does not maintain records of birds that died or were euthanized. 
 
Failure to maintain complete acquisition and disposition records interferes with the ability to accurately identify animals 
and trace their movement. 
 
The licensee shall make, keep, and maintain records that fully and correctly disclose information concerning regulated 
animals purchased, born, hatched or otherwise acquired, as well as animals transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise 
disposed.  
To be corrected by May 19, 2026. 
3.153(a)(1)(iii) 
   
Primary enclosures.

During the inspection, 6 budgies were observed outside their enclosures and flying free inside the Big Barn.  
 
Enclosures that fail to safely contain birds may result in escape, injury, or death of the animal. Birds that are outside of 
their enclosure are at increased risk of predation, possible restricted access to food, water, shelter, and potential non-
recovery. 
 
Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they contain the birds securely.  
To be corrected by May 19. 2026. 
3.158(a)(1)  Repeat
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The facility failed to prevent the accumulation excreta and food waste from the primary enclosures housing birds. In one 
enclosure containing a pair of breeding budgies the droppings were piled up the level of the perch (approximately 4 
inches). In another enclosure there was a mound of fecal approximately 2 inches in diameter that was covered in a green 
hairy mold-like substance. Approximately 70 percent of the enclosures in Willow barn and the Big Barn were covered with 
a layer of caked-on excreta and food waste. There were approximately 10 cages with dead budgies in various states of 
decay  
 
Failure to clean accumulations of waste materials can increase disease hazards, attract insects, pests, and be a source of 
odors. 
 
Excreta and food waste must be removed from the primary enclosures and from under and from around primary 
enclosures as often as necessary to prevent excessive accumulation of feces and food waste, to reduce disease hazards, 
insects, pests, and odors.  
To be corrected by May 19, 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Geoffrey Olsen, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER 
Emily Roberson n
